coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dots-referenced metal-organic frameworks for chemical sensing of tumor/mood biomarker 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid in human urine: Covalent grafting blue-emitting carbon dots onto red-emitting MOF
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fei, YuLang
Sun, Kai
Liu, Liang
coredata.dc:description: 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is a metabolite for 5-hydroxytryptamine which is excreted in urine and reflects human homeostatic sate. Thus, its monitoring is of great important for clinical diagnosis. In this work, blue-emitting carbon dots (BCD) were firstly synthesize and then covalently grafted onto red-emitting MOF (EuBTC), resulting in a composite sensing platform (BCD@EuBTC) with two emission bands (blue emission peaking at 441 nm and red emission peaking at 616 nm). This composite structure was characterized by means of XRD, IR, TGA, N2 adsorption/desorption, and SEM/TEM. It was found that BCD was grafted on EuBTC surface, not loaded in its micropores, with doping level of 5.02 wt%. 5-HIAA replaced and released the BCD in BCD@EuBTC. The released BCD showed strong blue emission. In the meanwhile, EuBTC red emission was quenched by 5-HIAA thermal relaxation. As a consequence, a ratiometric sensing signal was observed for 5-HIAA. A linear working calibration curve was fitted as F/F0 = 0.588 + 1.598*[5-HIAA], R2 = 0.999, with detection of limit (LOD) determined as 0.3 μM, working region of 0.3–70 μM, and good selectivity. The practical sensing performance of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA in human urine was confirmed, with recovery of 103%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Eu MOF
Blue-emitting
Red-emitting
5-HIAA
Urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Grafting between BCD and EuBTC confirmed by IR
Micromorphology revealed by N2 adsorption/desorption and SEM/TEM
Practical performance of BCD@EuBTC in urine: Establishing a warning signal for 5-HIAA abnormal level

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank below funds, including Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi (Program No. 2021JQ-881); Talent Research of Xijing University, Grant/Award Number: XJ21B13 for their support and help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Powder XRD curves of BCD@EuBTC, BCD and EuBTC, simulated XRD peaks of EuBTC nanocrystal (CCDC 290771).
IR spectra of BCD@EuBTC, as-synthesized BCD and EuBTC.
TGA (a) and DGT (b) curves of BCD@EuBTC and EuBTC.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of BCD@EuBTC and EuBTC.

SEM image of EuBTC (a), micropores of EuBTC and their size determined by single crystal (b), TEM images of BCD (c and d), SEM images of BCD@EuBTC (e and f). For a magnified photo of Fig. 5b, please check Supporting Information.
The emission spectra of BCD@EuBTC upon increasing 5-HIAA concentrations from 0 to 70 μM. Inset: emission intensity variation at 441 nm and 616 nm.
The emission intensity monitoring at 441 nm and 616 nm after adding 5-HIAA (70 μM) at ambient condition.
BCD@EuBTC emission spectra upon interferents (50 μM). Inset: corresponding F441/I616 values. 0, blank; 1, 5-HIAA; 2, GLY; 3, ASP; 4, MET; 5, TGA; 6, URE; 7, CYS; 8, GLU; 9, Na+.
BCD@EuBTC dispersed in human urine (a) and human urine + 80 μM of 5-HIAA (b).
The synthetic strategy and working mechanism of BCD@EuBTC.
A possible sensing mechanism of BCD@EuBTC towards 5-HIAA.
5-HIAA sensing comparison by HLPC and BCD@EuBTC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, N/A means lower than LOD.
b, since this value was out of the effective working region (0.3–70 μM), this sample was diluted.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dots-referenced metal-organic frameworks for chemical sensing of tumor/mood biomarker 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid in human urine: Covalent grafting blue-emitting carbon dots onto red-emitting MOF

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical College, Xijing University, Xi 'an 710123, Shaanxi Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical College, Xijing University, Xi 'an 710123, Shaanxi Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Affiliated Hospital of Nanyang Medical College, Nanyang 473061, Henan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Nanyang Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Affiliated Hospital of Nanyang Medical College, Nanyang 473061, Henan Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is a metabolite for 5-hydroxytryptamine which is excreted in urine and reflects human homeostatic sate. Thus, its monitoring is of great important for clinical diagnosis. In this work, blue-emitting carbon dots (BCD) were firstly synthesize and then covalently grafted onto red-emitting MOF (EuBTC), resulting in a composite sensing platform (BCD@EuBTC) with two emission bands (blue emission peaking at 441 nm and red emission peaking at 616 nm). This composite structure was characterized by means of XRD, IR, TGA, N2 adsorption/desorption, and SEM/TEM. It was found that BCD was grafted on EuBTC surface, not loaded in its micropores, with doping level of 5.02 wt%. 5-HIAA replaced and released the BCD in BCD@EuBTC. The released BCD showed strong blue emission. In the meanwhile, EuBTC red emission was quenched by 5-HIAA thermal relaxation. As a consequence, a ratiometric sensing signal was observed for 5-HIAA. A linear working calibration curve was fitted as F/F0 = 0.588 + 1.598*[5-HIAA], R2 = 0.999, with detection of limit (LOD) determined as 0.3 μM, working region of 0.3–70 μM, and good selectivity. The practical sensing performance of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA in human urine was confirmed, with recovery of 103%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The neurotransmitters and their metabolites in blood plasma and urine are usually considered as bio-markers for various diseases or disorders of human body, such as hypertension/hypotension, neuroblastoma, pheochromocytoma and so on [1–3]. 5-HIAA (short for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is a metabolite for 5-hydroxytryptamine (widely known as serotonin, 5-HT). It is excreted in urine and reflects human homeostatic sate. For example, 5-HIAA concentration in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) can be used to predict suicide risk. A low level indicates aggressive behaviors, high suicidal risk and mood disorder in adult patients, and a high risk of sudden infant (younger than 12 months) death syndrome [4–6]. An increasing 5-HIAA/5HT ratio has been related to natural aging, and plasma 5-HIAA has been nominated as a biomarker for appendices, carcinoid tumors, and intestinal cancer (8.87 μg/L for patient vs. 1.90 μg/L for healthy people) [7]. Generally speaking, 5-HIAA level is a biomarker for the starting stage of tumors and body disorders. Thus, its monitoring is of great important for clinical diagnosis [8,9].

There have been several analytical methods for the detection of 5-HIAA, such as fluorescent spectrophotometry, capillary electrophoresis, liquid/gas chromatography, mass spectrometry, and infra-red spectroscopy [10–13]. Among these methods, fluorescent spectrophotometry has been frequently mentioned owing to its virtues of easy-to-go operation procedure and low requirement for instrument. A typical optical sensing platform is composed of two functional components: a probe which generates sensing signals and a supporting host which holds and immobilizes probe molecules to allow fluent analyte diffusion and avoid concentration quenching [14]. The combination and of various probes and hosts leads to many sensing platforms, which pushes the development of both probe and supporting host. As a consequence, more and more probes and corresponding hosts have been reported [15–17].

In the exploration for probe, carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, are harvesting much research attention owing to their optical features and potential applications in sensing, catalysis, and bio-imaging [18–20]. CDs can be easily synthesized via hydrothermal method with biocompatible and economic starting materials, and their emission features are size-dependent and excitation-dependent, which endows CDs with controllable and variable emission features. The multiple functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -NH2) on CDs surface allow a good aqueous solubility and offers binding sites for further modification or immobilization [21].

In the exploration for host, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been widely reported since they well satisfy criteria for a supporting host such as uniform and porous microstructure, and stable mechanical structure [22,23]. The versatile organic linkers and metal ions endow MOFs with various structures which can be further modified to meet specific demands in applications. Their tunable pores and high porosity make MOFs a promising host for various guest species. Since the organic linkers and metal ions in MOFs are potential emitters, MOFs are usually emissive ones. A neat design for the energy transfer/competing between host (MOFs) and guest (probe) shall lead to emission variation upon analyte, showing sensing behavior. There have been efforts about MOFs and their application in optical sensing. For example, He and Cao reported the encapsulation and photo-luminescent property of [Eu(bpy)2]3+ in mesoporous material HMS [24]. Lama, Da, and Pal published a review on the engineering of MOF as ratiometric sensors [25]. Pedrosa and Tasiopoulos reported 2-D MOF for the detection of the vapours of nitroaromatics and volatile organic compounds [26]. Du and He summarized the tactics and perspectives of ratiometric fluorescence sensing of MOFs [27]. Li and coworkers published a review on lanthanide-functionalized MOFs as ratiometric luminescent sensors [28]. Lazarides and Tasiopoulos reported an approach of modifying MOF by an ion-exchange method [29]. Akhbari and coworkers published a review paper focusing on ion-exchange method which mentioned that it was an effective way to modulate structures and properties [30].

Guided by the above consideration, in this work, blue-emitting carbon dots (BCD) were firstly synthesize and then covalently grafted onto red-emitting MOF (EuBTC), resulting in a composite sensing platform (BCD@EuBTC) with two emission bands (blue emission peaking at 441 nm and red emission peaking at 616 nm). We chose red-emitting MOF (EuBTC) as host, owing to its good emission features, suitable porous structure, and more importantly, the unsaturated Eu ions on EuBTC crystal surface which offer coordination sites for BCD. This composite structure was characterized by means of XRD, IR, TGA, N2 adsorption/desorption, and SEM/TEM. It was found that 5-HIAA replaced and released the BCD in BCD@EuBTC. The released BCD showed strong blue emission. In the meanwhile, EuBTC red emission was quenched by 5-HIAA thermal relaxation. As a consequence, a ratiometric sensing signal was observed for 5-HIAA. A linear working calibration curve was fitted, with detection of limit (LOD) determined as 0.3 μM, working region of 0.3–70 μM, and good selectivity.

A schematic presentation for the synthetic strategy and working mechanism of BCD@EuBTC is shown in Scheme 1 . All starting chemicals (analytical grade) were commercially obtained and used as received, including 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaOAC), Eu(NO3)3, and cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000). Photophysical parameters were determined on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Powder XRD experiment was finished on a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer using Cu K radiation. IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 spectrophotometer (KBr method). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed by a Perkin-Elmer thermal analyzer. N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were recorded using a Noval000 analyzer under 77 K. SEM and TEM images were taken from a Hitachi S-4800 microscope (Hitachi, Japan) and a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), respectively. HLPC (high performance liquid chromatography) result was given by a Waters 600 instrument equipped with a XAqua C18 5 μm 100 Å column.

The red-emitting EuBTC was synthesized in accordance with a literature method [31]. To deionized water (30 mL), the following reagents were added, including Eu(NO3)3 (1 mmol) and NaOAc. After being treated with ultrasonic bath for 10 min, the solution was mixed with H3BTC solution in ethanol (1.0 mmol in 30 mL) and stirred vigorously. After being stirred for 2 h, white solid product was collected as EuBTC and dried in vacuum at 55 °C.

The blue-emitting BCD was prepared following a literature method [32]. Into deionized water (40 mL), the following reagents were added, including citric acid (1.68 g) and ethylenediamine (2.04 g). The resulting solution was sealed into a Teflon autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 190 °C for 5.5 h. After allowing the autoclave to be cooled to room temperature, the brown solution was sealed in a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) and dialyzed against deionized water (500 mL) for 5 times. Brown solid product was obtained by freeze drying (yield = 1.1 g), and then dispersed in deionized water as s stock solution with concentration of 10 mg/mL.

The final composite BCD@EuBTC was prepared following below procedure. The freshly-prepared EuBTC (0.1 g) was dispersed with BCD stock solution (10 mL) and stirred vigorously at ambient condition for 24 h. Then the mixture was sealed in a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) and dialyzed against deionized water (500 mL) for 5 times. The grafting procedure was repeated by 3 times. Light pale solid was obtained by freeze drying (yield = 0.09 g).

For sensing performance evaluation, 5-HIAA was dispersed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) solution, and BCD@EuBTC was added to form a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL. After being treated with ultrasonic bath for 3 min, steady emission spectrum was recorded upon excitation wavelength of 355 nm. Each spectrum was repeated three times to get a mean value for the calibration curve fitting.

For the practical sensing performance of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA in urine, fresh human urine samples were divided into two parts. The first part was mixed with BCD@EuBTC form a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL, and the other part was firstly mixed with 5-HIAA (80 μM) and then BCD@EuBTC was added to form a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL. Corresponding steady emission spectra were recorded after being treated with ultrasonic bath for 3 min. It should be pointed out that the 5-HIAA concentration in the second sample was out of the effective working region (0.3–70 μM), so this sample was diluted. Finally, the 5-HIAA level in these two samples were determined by HLPC method.

The microstructure of BCD@EuBTC is firstly discussed using its powder XRD shown in Fig. 1 . The powder XRD curves of as-synthesized BCD and as-synthesized EuBTC, along with the simulated XRD peaks of EuBTC crystal (CCDC 290771), are shown in Fig. 1 for comparison. It is observed that BCD sample has a broad XRD peak of ∼23°, which belongs to the orderly alignment of carbon atoms [33]. This result serves here as the evidence of successful synthesis of carbon dots. On the other hand, no such broad XRD peak is observed from BCD@EuBTC sample, which may be explained by the low doping concentration of BCD. In addition, the carbon dots are highly dispersed across EuBTC sample, instead of being aggregated together, which also decreases XRD intensity dramatically. The XRD peaks of BCD@EuBTC are nearly identical to those of EuBTC, which match the simulated XRD peaks of EuBTC rather well. This observation confirms that EuBTC has been successfully synthesized and its microstructure has been well preserved after grafting with BCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafting between BCD and EuBTC confirmed by IR
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, there are multiple functional groups (–COOH, –OH, –NH2) on carbon dots surface. The –COOH on BCD shall bond with the unsaturated Eu ion of EuBTC, forming a successful bonding between BCD and EuBTC. To confirm this assumption, the IR spectrum of BCD@EuBTC is recorded and compared to those of as-synthesized BCD and EuBTC samples, as shown in Fig. 2 . As for BCD sample, there are several characteristic bands peaking at 3430 cm−1 (–OH vibration from adsorbed H2O), 3031 cm−1 (N-H vibration), 1605 cm−1 (C=N vibration), and 1505 cm−1 (COO– vibration), respectively, which confirms the presence of these functional/active moieties in BCD. As for EuBTC, several characteristic peaks are observed, peaking at 3415 cm−1 (–COOH vibration), 1660 cm−1 (C=O vibration), 1115 cm−1 (Ar-H in-plane bending), and 1028 cm−1 (Ar-H off-plane bending). These IR peaks matches their chemical structure. As for BCD@EuBTC, the –COOH vibration (3420 cm−1) from EuBTC is well traced, and the IR peaks from N-H vibration (3031 cm−1) and C=N vibration (1605 cm−1) are still observed. But the IR peak from COO– vibration (1505 cm−1) is missing. In addition, the C=O vibration of BCD@EuBTC is blue shifted to 1715 cm−1, compared to that of EuBTC (1660 cm−1). It is thus confirmed that BCD has been successfully grafted onto EuBTC via a bond between carbon dot –COOH and unsaturated Eu ion of EuBTC.

After above confirming the successful grating between BCD and EuBTC in BCD@EuBTC, the BCD doping level in BCD@EuBTC is revealed by its TGA result shown in Fig. 3 . The TGA result of EuBTC is shown in Fig. 3 as well for comparison. As for EuBTC, a sharp weight loss is observed within 30–90 °C, which can be attributed to the thermal evaporation of adsorbed H2O. Then a smooth weight loss is observed within 100–370 °C, followed by a sharp weight loss within 370–490 °C, and finally reaches constant within 500–600 °C. The second weight loss region (370–490 °C) is obviously attributed to the thermal decomposition of EuBTC framework. As for BCD@EuBTC, similar weight loss regions are observed, with an additional weight loss step around 350 °C. This additional weight loss shall be attributed to the thermal release/decomposition of the grafted BCD in BCD@EuBTC. To assist weight loss assignment, DTG (derivative thermogravimetry) curves of EuBTC and BCD@EuBTC are plotted and shown in Fig. 3 as well. Clearly, the sharp endothermic procedure peaking at 342 °C corresponds to the thermal release/decomposition of the grafted BCD in BCD@EuBTC, while that at 422 °C corresponds to thermal decomposition of EuBTC framework. Within temperature region of 333–358 °C, BCD@EuBTC weight loss is 5.72 wt%, while EuBTC weight loss in the same temperature region is only 0.7 wt%. Thus, BCD doping level is mathematically calculated as 5.02 wt% in BCD@EuBTC. This doping level is generally lower than composite porous samples having dopant in their micropores [31]. In this case it is assumed that BCD dots are grafted on the surface of EuBTC nanocrystals, instead of being embedded into EuBTC micropores. This hypothesis will be confirmed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micromorphology revealed by N2 adsorption/desorption and SEM/TEM
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming at a better understanding on the microstructure of BCD@EuBTC, its N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms are recorded and compared to those of as-synthesized EuBTC. As shown in Fig. 4 , the as-synthesized EuBTC shows Type-I isotherms, which is consistent with its micropores ∼2 nm [31]. The obvious N2 intake at low pressure (<0.1P/P0) suggests the presence of these micropores. Similar Type-I isotherms are observed for BCD@EuBTC, which means that these micropores have been well preserved. The surface area and pore size of the as-synthesized EuBTC are determined as 714.1 m2g−1 and 1.92 nm, while those of BCD@EuBTC are slightly decreased as 707.3 m2g−1 and 1.90 nm for Tb:Eu(BTC), respectively. This result suggests that the micropores in EuBTC have been preserved after grafting with BCD, instead of being occupied by carbon dots. This observation is consistent with the above assumption in Section 3.1.3.

A visual understanding on the micromorphology of BCD@EuBTC is explored via its SEM/TEM images shown in Fig. 5 . Those of the as-synthesized EuBTC and BCD are shown in Fig. 5 for comparison. As for the as-synthesized EuBTC nanocrystals, they are rod-liked ones with length of ∼500 nm and width of ∼60 nm. Regardless of their random alignment, there is no conjunction or knot in them, indicating that there exists an optimal growth orientation. The TEM image of BCD suggests that these carbon dots are round ones with diameter of ∼6 nm. These carbon dots are too large to be loaded into the micropores of EuBTC (∼2 nm) which is determined by both N2 adsorption/desorption result (mentioned above in Section 3.1.4) and single crystal structure of EuBTC (CCDC 290771, as plotted by Fig. 5). In other words, BCD can only be grafted on EuBTC surface, not into EuBTC micropores. This statement is confirmed by the SEM images of BCD@EuBTC shown in Fig. 5. It is obvious that carbon dots are distributed on EuBTC surface.

The emission spectra of BCD@EuBTC upon increasing 5-HIAA concentrations from 0 to 70 μM are recorded and shown in Fig. 6 . Emission spectra are recorded after adding 5-HIAA and then being treated by ultrasonic bath for 3 min to allow reaction equilibrium. In the absence of 5-HIAA, a weak blue emission peaking at 441 nm is observed, which has been assigned as the emission from carbon dots BCD [32]. There are two more emission bands, peaking at 594 nm and 616 nm, respectively, which are assigned as 5D0→7F1,2 transitions of Eu(III) ions. It is obvious that the two functional moieties of BCD@EuBTC, BCD and EuBTC, have preserved their individual emission features. Regardless of the above confirmed grafting between BCD and EuBTC, no emission from exciplex or adduct (between BCD and EuBTC). On the other hand, the BCD blue emission is rather weak, compared to the red emission from EuBTC. Considering the reported high emission quantum yield of BCD (∼70%), it is assumed that there can be an energy transfer (ET) procedure from BCD to EuBTC, which explains the weak blue emission of BCD@EuBTC at 441 nm. With increasing 5-HIAA concentrations, the BCD blue emission is gradually increased, while the 5D0→7F1,2 transitions of Eu(III) ions are correspondingly decreased, resulting in a ratiometric signal. No obvious spectral shift is observed during the addition of 5-HIAA, which ensures signal reliability.

Based on the above steady emission spectra of BCD@EuBTC against increasing 5-HIAA concentrations, Stern-Volmer equation is applied here to analyze the emission intensity variation, whose general form is described as Equation 1 [31]. Here I0 means the initial emission intensity in the absence of quencher, I is emission intensity, C is constant, Ksv is Stern-Volmer constant (sensitivity), and [analyte] means the concentration of analyte.
(1) I0/I = C + Ksv[analyte]

By applying Equation 1 to the red emission intensity at 616 nm, corresponding I0/I plots against [5-HIAA] are shown in Fig. 7 . A non-linear calibration curve is observed, which fails to obey Equation 1. This result suggests that the decreasing red emission intensity of EuBTC is not caused by a direct quenching or energy competing on the emissive center (Eu3+), which will be discussed below in detail. On the other hand, by applying Equation 1 to the blue emission intensity at 616 nm, a linear working curve is observed, as shown in Fig. 7. Corresponding fitting equation is fitted as F/F0 = 0.588 + 1.598*[5-HIAA], R2 = 0.999. Here F0 means the emission intensity at 441 nm without 5-HIAA, and F means emission intensity at 441 nm. Following a literature method (3σ), the detection of limit (LOD) is determined as 0.30 μM. The observation of this linear working curve means that the emission intensity is proportional to the quantity of 5-HIAA molecule. There can be another ratiometric signal formed by the emission intensity values at 441 nm (F441) and 616 nm (I616), described as F441/I616. These plots, however, still fail to obey a linear working curve, as shown in Fig. 7. This is because the red emission intensity at 616 nm is not directly dominated by the quantity of 5-HIAA molecule, as above mentioned, leading to this non-linear working curve [34,35].

It has been above found out that the blue emission intensity at 441 nm is proportional to the quantity of 5-HIAA molecule, while the emission intensity at 616 nm is not. The ratio of F441/I616 fails to obey a linear correlation. This result suggests that these two emissive centers are independent ones, with no direct energy competing with each other. After carefully considering the molecular structures of BCD, EuBTC, and 5-HIAA, a possible sensing mechanism is proposed as follows and depicted as Scheme 2 . In the absence of 5-HIAA, BCD grafts with EuBTC via a bond between carbon dot –COOH and unsaturated Eu ion of EuBTC, as above mentioned, and BCD blue emission is quenched by its energy transfer to EuBTC. Upon the presence of 5-HIAA, its –COOH competes with BCD and tends to replace BCD owing to the large size (heavy weight) of BCD which makes the BCD-COOH-EuBTC bond fragile. In other words, 5-HIAA molecules replace the grafted BCD in BCD@EuBTC. In the meanwhile, the released BCD creases to transfer energy to EuBTC, showing improved blue emission. As for EuBTC, after being grafted with 5-HIAA, the geometric relaxation of –NH and –OH moieties tends to quench EuBTC emission, resulting in decreased red emission. For a tentative confirmation on this sensing mechanism, the SEM image of recycled BCD@EuBTC after 5-HIAA sensing is shown in Supporting Information. The carbon dots on sample surface are much less than those on the original BCD@EuBTC. As a consequence, the sensing mechanism of “5-HIAA replacing and releasing BCD” is confirmed.

After above confirming the sensing mechanism of releasing BCD by 5-HIAA, the response time of this procedure should be determined. As shown in Fig. 8 , the emission intensity at 441 nm and 616 nm is monitored after adding 5-HIAA. It is observed that the blue emission at 441 nm increases gradually after adding 5-HIAA and reaches constant after 180 s, while the red emission at 616 nm correspondingly quenches and reaches constant after 150 s. For signal reliability, the time for reaction equilibrium was set as 3 min, as mentioned in Section 2.5. This experiment was carried out at ambient condition. According to our analysis about sensing mechanism of “5-HIAA replacing and releasing BCD”, this replacing/releasing mechanism should be sensitive to temperature, which means that a low temperature shall slow this procedure down, and a high one shall facilitate this procedure. On the other hand, a varying temperature can lead to emission variation of both BCD and EuBTC, which makes the sensing procedure complicated. Thus, to simplify sensing procedure, the sensing operation should be taken at a fixed temperature.

The sensing selectivity of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA over competing species is tentatively discussed by its emission spectra upon the presence of following reagents (50 μM), including GLY (glycine), ASP (aspartic acid), MET (methionine), TGA (thioglycolic acid), URE (urine), CYS (cysteine), GLU (glucose), and Na+. It is observed from Fig. 9 that only the presence of 5-HIAA leads to an obvious emission increase at 441 nm, all these mentioned competing species fail to do so. Even though some reagents lead to emission quenching at 616 nm, such as TGA and CYS, no increased blue emission is observed. The good sensing selectivity of BCD@EuBTC towards 5-HIAA is thus confirmed. A possible explanation for this good selectivity is the sensing mechanism of “5-HIAA replacing and releasing BCD”. There exists a conjugation plane in 5-HIAA which stabilizes its coordination with Eu3+, while these mentioned competing species have short and un-conjugated chains, which makes their coordination with Eu3+ unstable, and thus fails to replace and release carbon dots grafted on BCD@EuBTC. Some of them even quench the BCD emission. As a consequence, low F441/I616 values are observed for these competing species. In addition, according to our analysis about sensing mechanism of “5-HIAA replacing and releasing BCD”, this the selectivity should be sensitive to temperature. A low temperature shall slow the replacing/releasing procedure down, which results in weakened signal at low temperature. While, at a high temperature, the replacing/releasing procedure shall be facilitated and activated by thermal vibration, resulting in fake signals generated by these interferents. Moreover, the varying temperature affects the emission intensity of both BCD and EuBTC. Thus, to ensure signal reliability, sensing operation should be carried out at ambient condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical performance of BCD@EuBTC in urine: Establishing a warning signal for 5-HIAA abnormal level
ce:section.ce:para: As above mentioned, F441/I616 against increasing 5-HIAA concentrations follow a non-linear working curve. Low F441/I616 values are observed when [5-HIAA] is lower than 42 μM (F441/I616 < 0.57). When [5-HIAA] is higher than 50 μM, F441/I616 value is dramatically increased (F441/I616 = 0.89 at 50 μM, F441/I616 = 5.82 at 70 μM). This feature can be designed as a “warning signal” for 5-HIAA abnormal level. In other words, when [5-HIAA] falls in the normal concentration region (10.5–42.0 μM in urine sample), a low F441/I616 value is observed. Upon an abnormal 5-HIAA concentration, F441/I616 value is obviously increased, to indicate this abnormal level and show a warning signal. This feature actually endows BCD@EuBTC with a practical application in human urine detection.

To confirm the practical sensing performance, 5-HIAA concentration is determined in human urine samples. As shown in Table 1 , BCD@EuBTC gives precise result and recovery of ∼103%. The positive deviations shall be explained by the competing species which quenches Eu red emission and thus leads to sensing errors. As shown in Fig. 10 , for a healthy human urine sample with 5-HIAA concentration lower than 40 μM, BCD@EuBTC shows red emission. After adding more 5-HIAA (+80 μM) into human urine sample, blue emission is observed. This obvious emission color change can be easily observed by naked-eyes. Thus, the practical application of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA sensing is finally confirmed.

To sum up, a composite structure BCD@EuBTC was constructed by grafting BCD onto EuBTC via a bond between BCD-COOH and the unsaturated Eu ion of EuBTC. This composite structure was carefully characterized by means of XRD, IR, TGA, N2 adsorption/desorption, and SEM/TEM. It was found that BCD was grafted on EuBTC surface, not loaded in its micropores, with doping level of 5.02 wt%. 5-HIAA replaced and released the BCD in BCD@EuBTC. The released BCD showed strong blue emission. In the meanwhile, EuBTC red emission was quenched by 5-HIAA thermal relaxation. As a consequence, a ratiometric sensing signal was observed for 5-HIAA. A linear working calibration curve was fitted as F/F0 = 0.588 + 1.598*[5-HIAA], R2 = 0.999, with detection of limit (LOD) determined as 0.3 μM, working region of 0.3–70 μM, and good selectivity. The practical sensing performance of BCD@EuBTC for 5-HIAA in human urine was confirmed, with recovery of 103%. For future improvement, BCD doping level should be improved so that sensitivity can be improved.
YuLang Fei: Writing – original draft. Kai Sun: Supervision. Liang Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
